lessons.title: One Computer by Hand vs a Whole Fleet
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Setting up one computer by hand is fine. You log in, install a few programs, edit a couple of files, start a service, and you are done. But now imagine you must set up fifty computers the *exact same way* - and keep them that way for years, even after one gets wiped and rebuilt. Doing that by hand is slow, and worse, every machine ends up a little bit different. This lesson is about that gap.

**Build.** A group of machines you manage together is often called a **fleet** (just a friendly word for "many servers"). The trouble with hand-setup is that the recipe lives only in your memory or in scattered notes. Did you remember every step on machine #37? Did a teammate do it the same way? When something breaks at 2am, can anyone rebuild it exactly? A **server** here just means a computer that runs a service for other computers (a website, a database, and so on).

The modern fix is to stop relying on *remembered* steps and instead make the setup **reproducible**: written down so precisely that a tool can redo it perfectly, the same way, every time, on every machine. "By hand" does not scale; "reproducible" does.

**Recap.** One machine by hand is fine. Many machines, kept identical and rebuildable, is the real job - and the goal is *reproducible*, not *remembered*.

items.prompt: In this module, what does the word "fleet" mean?
choices: A single very powerful computer
A group of many computers (servers) you manage together
A type of network cable
A backup copy of one server
items.explanation: A fleet is simply many machines managed together. The whole challenge of this module is keeping many machines consistent, not running one big one.

items.prompt: Why is setting up ONE computer by hand usually fine?
choices: Because one computer cannot run services
Because it is a small, one-time job you can do and verify directly
Because hand setup is always faster than any tool
Because a single computer never needs to be rebuilt
items.explanation: For one machine, doing it by hand is a small job you can check yourself. The problems show up only when you must repeat it identically across many machines over time.

items.prompt: What is the main problem with setting up fifty machines by hand?
choices: It is impossible to install software on more than one machine
It is slow and the machines drift into being slightly different from each other
Servers cannot be connected to a network
Each machine needs a different operating system
items.explanation: By hand, you cannot realistically repeat every step perfectly fifty times, so machines end up subtly different ('drift'). Consistency at scale is the hard part.

items.prompt: A server, as used here, is best described as:
choices: A person who manages computers
A computer that provides a service (like a website or database) to others
A kind of password
A backup tape
items.explanation: A server is just a computer running a service for other computers. The word does not imply anything fancier than that.

items.prompt: The module says the goal is to be "reproducible, not remembered." What does that mean?
choices: Memorize the steps so well you never need notes
Write the setup down so precisely that it can be redone perfectly every time, instead of relying on memory
Reproduce the hardware exactly, buying identical computers
Remember to back up your servers every night
items.explanation: 'Reproducible' means the setup is captured precisely enough to redo perfectly and automatically. Relying on a person's memory ('remembered') is exactly what fails at scale.

items.prompt: What goes wrong when the only record of how a server was set up is in one person's head?
choices: The server runs faster but uses more power
If that person forgets, is unavailable, or leaves, the exact setup can be lost and hard to rebuild
The server automatically deletes its own files
Nothing - human memory is the most reliable record
items.explanation: Knowledge trapped in one head is fragile: people forget, take vacations, and leave. A written, repeatable description survives all of that.

items.prompt: Which everyday comparison best captures "by hand" vs "reproducible"?
choices: A calculator vs an abacus
One chef cooking from memory vs a written recipe any cook can follow
A car vs a bicycle
A phone call vs a letter
items.explanation: A written recipe can be followed by anyone, repeatedly, with the same result - just like written setup-as-code. One chef's memory is the fragile 'by hand' version.

items.prompt: Why does "by hand" not scale, while "reproducible" does?
choices: Hands get tired but computers do not
A repeatable, written setup can be applied to 1 or 1000 machines the same way, while manual steps must be re-done and re-checked each time
Reproducible setups need no electricity
Manual setup only works on weekends
items.explanation: Once the setup is written precisely, applying it to many machines is cheap and consistent. Manual work grows with every machine and invites mistakes - that is what 'does not scale' means.

items.prompt: Which statement is the honest scope of this whole module?
choices: By the end you will be a senior expert in every tool mentioned
It is an orientation: the goal is that the vocabulary and the landscape feel familiar, not mastery of each tool
It teaches only one tool and nothing else exists
It replaces the need to ever set up a machine by hand again
items.explanation: This module is orientation. It aims to make the words and the big picture familiar so they are not intimidating later - deep mastery of each tool comes with hands-on practice.

lessons.title: SSH: Controlling a Machine From Far Away
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Before you can manage many machines, you need a safe way to reach into *one* machine that is somewhere else - maybe in another building or a data center far away - and run commands on it as if you were sitting in front of it. The standard tool for that is **SSH**.

**Build.** SSH stands for **Secure Shell**. Think of it as a locked, private phone line into another computer's command line. Everything you send and receive is **encrypted** (scrambled so only the two ends can read it), so no one listening in between can see your commands or steal what comes back. You typically type something like `ssh you@server.example.com`, and you land at that machine's prompt.

How does the remote machine know it is really you? You *can* use a password, but the safer, standard way is an **SSH key pair**: two matching files. The **private key** stays secret on your computer and proves your identity. The matching **public key** is placed on the server (it acts like a lock that only your private key opens). Keys are stronger than passwords, and - crucially for automation - they let a *tool* log in without a human there to type a password.

When you manage many machines, you can pre-write connection details in a small file (`~/.ssh/config`) so that `ssh web-01` just works. Some machines are not reachable directly; you reach them by first hopping through one gateway machine, called a **bastion** or **jump host** - the single guarded door into a private network.

**Recap.** SSH is an encrypted, password-or-key login into a remote machine's command line. Key pairs are safer than passwords and let tools log in automatically - the foundation everything else here builds on.

items.prompt: What does SSH let you do?
choices: Speed up a computer's hard drive
Securely log in to a remote computer and run commands on it as if you were there
Translate code from one language to another
Back up files to a USB stick
items.explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) gives you a remote command line on another machine. It is the basic way to control a computer that is somewhere else.

items.prompt: What does the "Secure" in Secure Shell refer to?
choices: The connection is encrypted, so eavesdroppers cannot read it
The remote computer is kept in a locked room
It only works between computers owned by the same person
It scans the remote machine for viruses
items.explanation: SSH encrypts the traffic between the two ends, so anyone intercepting it in between just sees scrambled data. That is the 'secure' part.

items.prompt: In an SSH key pair, which key must you keep secret, and where does it stay?
choices: The public key, stored on the server
The private key, kept secret on your own computer
Both keys are public and shared freely
Neither - keys are just longer passwords typed each time
items.explanation: The private key stays secret on your machine and proves who you are. The public key is the non-secret 'lock' you place on servers. Never share the private key.

items.prompt: Why are SSH keys preferred over passwords for automation?
choices: Keys are shorter and easier to type
Keys let a tool log in without a human present to type a password, and they are stronger than passwords
Passwords do not work over a network at all
Keys make the connection unencrypted, which is faster
items.explanation: Automation runs with no human at the keyboard, so it needs login that does not require typing a password. Key-based auth provides that, and is more secure too.

items.prompt: What is a bastion (jump host)?
choices: A backup server that stores copies of files
A single guarded gateway machine you hop through to reach machines on a private network
A faster type of SSH key
The main database of a company
items.explanation: A bastion is the one exposed 'door' into an otherwise private network. You connect to it first, then on to the internal machines - keeping the rest off the open internet.

items.prompt: What is the point of an `~/.ssh/config` file?
choices: It stores your passwords in plain text for convenience
It saves connection details (host, user, jump host) so short commands like `ssh web-01` just work
It encrypts your hard drive
It lists every command you have ever run
items.explanation: The SSH config file records per-host settings once, so you do not retype them. It is about convenience and consistency, not storing secrets.

items.prompt: The public key acts most like which everyday object?
choices: A diary you keep hidden
A lock you can hand out, which only your private key opens
A spare house that anyone can live in
A phone number that changes daily
items.explanation: You can freely place the public key (the 'lock') on many servers; only the matching private key (which you keep) can open it. That is why sharing the public key is safe.

items.prompt: If someone steals your private key, what is the risk?
choices: None - the private key is useless without your password manager
They could log in to any machine that trusts your matching public key, impersonating you
Your screen brightness changes
The public key automatically deletes itself
items.explanation: The private key proves identity, so a stolen private key lets an attacker log in as you wherever the public key is installed. That is exactly why it must stay secret (and ideally be protected by a passphrase).

items.prompt: Why is SSH described as the foundation for managing a fleet?
choices: Because it stores all your code
Because the tools that configure many machines reach those machines over SSH
Because it replaces the operating system
Because it is the only way to write a website
items.explanation: Fleet tools need a secure, scriptable way into each machine. SSH (with keys) is that channel, which is why everything else here sits on top of it.

lessons.title: Infrastructure as Code: Write It Down, Let a Tool Apply It
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You can now reach a machine over SSH and type commands. But typing is still *remembering*. The next leap is to write the setup down as a file - as **code** - and let a tool apply it for you. This idea is called **Infrastructure as Code** (often shortened to **IaC**).

**Build.** "Infrastructure" just means the machines and the things on them (servers, the software they run, their settings). "As code" means you describe all of that in plain text files that you save, share, and track - exactly like the source code of a program. A teammate can read it, review a change before it happens, and keep a history of what changed and when.

Compare two recipes for a dish. One lives only in a single chef's head; if that chef is out, the dish changes or disappears. The other is *written down*, so any cook can produce the same dish every time. Infrastructure as Code is the written recipe for your machines.

There is also a powerful shift in *how* you describe things. Instead of writing step-by-step instructions ("do this, then this") - called **imperative** - good IaC tools are mostly **declarative**: you state the *end result you want* ("the web server should be installed and running") and the tool figures out the steps to make reality match. You describe *what*, not *how*. The tool then applies it **automatically** (no human typing each command) and **repeatably** (the same way every time).

**Recap.** Infrastructure as Code means writing your machines' setup as saved, reviewable text and letting a tool apply it. Declarative tools let you say *what you want* and handle the *how* - the written-recipe approach to running computers.

items.prompt: What does "Infrastructure as Code" (IaC) mean?
choices: Writing computer games in a special language
Describing your machines and their setup in saved text files, like source code, so a tool can apply it
Building physical server rooms out of concrete
Encrypting all your files automatically
items.explanation: IaC captures the setup of machines as text you keep and version like code. A tool then reads it and makes the machines match - that is the whole idea.

items.prompt: Infrastructure as Code is most like which of these?
choices: A chef who keeps the recipe only in their memory
A written recipe that any cook can follow to get the same dish every time
Tasting a dish and guessing the ingredients
Throwing out the recipe after cooking once
items.explanation: The written recipe is the point: it can be read, reviewed, and re-followed for the same result. The chef's memory is the fragile, by-hand approach IaC replaces.

items.prompt: Why is keeping infrastructure 'as code' (in text files) valuable for a team?
choices: Text files run faster than other files
Teammates can read it, review changes before they happen, and keep a history of what changed
It hides the setup so no one can see it
It removes the need for any servers
items.explanation: Because it is just text like source code, you get review, sharing, and history - the same benefits version control gives normal code. That is a big reason teams adopt IaC.

items.prompt: What is the difference between 'declarative' and 'imperative' descriptions?
choices: Declarative is written in English; imperative is written in numbers
Declarative states the end result you want; imperative lists the step-by-step actions to take
Declarative is slower; imperative is always faster
They mean the same thing
items.explanation: Declarative = describe the desired end state ('nginx installed and running'). Imperative = spell out each step. IaC tools lean declarative so they can work out the 'how' for you.

items.prompt: A declarative tool lets you describe mainly what?
choices: The exact order of every keystroke
What the final result should be, leaving the tool to work out the steps
Which employee should do the work
The price of the hardware
items.explanation: Declarative means you say 'what I want the end state to be' and the tool determines the steps. That is the opposite of dictating each action yourself.

items.prompt: In IaC, what does it mean that the tool applies your description "automatically"?
choices: A human still types every command, just faster
The tool carries out the needed steps itself, without a person typing each command
It only works if you are watching the screen
It needs you to approve each file copy by hand
items.explanation: Automatic means the tool does the work itself from your written description - no human typing each step. That is what makes it scale across many machines.

items.prompt: How does IaC connect to the earlier 'reproducible, not remembered' idea?
choices: It does not - IaC is about speed only
The code IS the reproducible written record; the tool re-applies it the same way instead of relying on memory
It replaces servers with people's memories
It only works the very first time and never again
items.explanation: IaC is the concrete form of 'reproducible': the setup is written down and re-applied identically, so nothing depends on someone remembering the steps.

items.prompt: Which of these is the best one-line summary of IaC's payoff?
choices: You never need to understand Linux again
'Set this machine up exactly like this' becomes a saved, re-runnable file instead of a list of remembered commands
Servers no longer need electricity
All passwords are stored in the code for convenience
items.explanation: IaC turns 'set it up like this' into a durable, re-runnable artifact. (Storing passwords in the code would be a serious mistake - secrets are handled separately, as a later lesson covers.)

items.prompt: Does using IaC mean you no longer need to understand the underlying Linux tools?
choices: Yes - IaC replaces all Linux knowledge
No - you still describe real Linux actions (install packages, edit files, start services); IaC organizes and automates them, it does not erase them
Yes - the tool invents its own operating system
No - because IaC only works on Windows
items.explanation: IaC automates real Linux work; it does not remove the need to understand it. You are still installing packages, managing files, and running services - just describing them as code.

lessons.title: Ansible: Reading a Checklist Over SSH
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Infrastructure as Code is the idea; **Ansible** is one of the most popular and beginner-friendly tools that *does* it. Ansible reads a checklist you write and makes your machines match it - over the SSH connection you already understand.

**Build.** Ansible's biggest friendliness win is that it is **agentless**. Many tools require you to first install a special helper program (an "agent") on every machine before you can manage it. Ansible does not. It connects to each machine over plain **SSH** and does its work. The rule of thumb: *if you can SSH into it, Ansible can manage it* - nothing to install first.

You tell Ansible two things:

- A **playbook**: a file (written in **YAML**, a simple indented text format) that lists *what each machine should look like* - the checklist. Each item is a small statement of desired state, like "the package `nginx` should be installed" and "the `nginx` service should be running."
- An **inventory**: the list of *which machines* to apply it to, often in named groups like `webservers`.

```yaml
# site.yml - run with: ansible-playbook -i inventory site.yml
- hosts: webservers          # which machines (from the inventory)
  become: true               # act as administrator (like sudo)
  tasks:
    - name: Make sure nginx is installed
      ansible.builtin.package:
        name: nginx
        state: present        # desired state: installed
    - name: Make sure nginx is running
      ansible.builtin.service:
        name: nginx
        state: started        # desired state: running
```

Read each task as *an assertion of how things should be*, not a script line to blindly execute. The machine running Ansible (your laptop or a central server) is the **control node**; the managed machines need only SSH access.

**Recap.** Ansible applies Infrastructure as Code with no agent to install - just SSH. A **playbook** says *what* each machine should look like; an **inventory** says *which* machines to apply it to.

items.prompt: What is Ansible, in one sentence?
choices: A web browser for servers
A popular tool that reads a checklist you write and makes machines match it, working over SSH
A programming language like Python
A type of computer hardware
items.explanation: Ansible is a configuration tool: you describe the desired setup in a file and it makes the machines match, connecting over SSH. It is one concrete way to do Infrastructure as Code.

items.prompt: What does it mean that Ansible is "agentless"?
choices: It runs without any network connection
You do NOT have to install a special helper program on each managed machine first; it uses SSH
It works without electricity
It cannot manage more than one machine at a time
items.explanation: Agentless means no pre-installed helper ('agent') is required on the targets. If you can SSH in, Ansible can manage it - which makes getting started easy.

items.prompt: How does Ansible reach the machines it manages?
choices: Through a custom protocol you must install everywhere first
Over standard SSH, the same secure remote-login channel from the earlier lesson
By plugging in a USB cable
Through the machine's web browser
items.explanation: Ansible uses ordinary SSH to connect to each machine. That is why good SSH key setup (from the SSH lesson) matters so much for it.

items.prompt: What is an Ansible playbook?
choices: The list of which machines to manage
A file (in YAML) describing what each machine should look like - the checklist of desired state
A password vault
A log of past errors
items.explanation: A playbook is the 'what should be true' checklist, written in YAML. (The list of WHICH machines is the inventory - a different file.)

items.prompt: What is an Ansible inventory?
choices: The checklist of tasks to perform
The list of which machines (often in named groups) to apply the playbook to
The amount of disk space left
A backup of the operating system
items.explanation: The inventory answers 'which machines?' - often grouped, e.g. 'webservers'. The playbook answers 'what should be done?'. Keep the two roles straight.

items.prompt: In the task `state: present` for a package, what is `present` describing?
choices: The order tasks run in
The desired end state - that the package should be installed - which Ansible then enforces
A raw shell command to execute
The SSH username
items.explanation: `state: present` declares the desired condition (installed). Ansible checks current state and installs only if needed. It is a statement of desired state, not a command to run blindly.

items.prompt: Why should you read an Ansible task as an "assertion of state" rather than a script line?
choices: Because tasks are written in a secret code
Because each task declares how things SHOULD be, and Ansible only acts if reality differs from that
Because tasks always run in random order
Because tasks cannot install software
items.explanation: Declarative tasks state the desired condition; Ansible compares and changes only what is off. That mindset (assert state, not 'do step') is key to using it well.

items.prompt: What is the 'control node' in an Ansible setup?
choices: The most important managed server
The machine that runs Ansible and connects out to the managed machines
A piece of network hardware
The database that stores all the data
items.explanation: The control node is where Ansible runs (your laptop or a central server). It reaches out over SSH to the managed machines, which need no agent.

items.prompt: A teammate says: "Just SSH in and re-run the setup shell script we wrote." Why might an Ansible playbook be preferable for many machines?
choices: Shell scripts cannot install packages
A plain script may not be safe to re-run (it can double-apply or fail on a half-set-up box), while a declarative playbook just brings each machine to the stated end state
Ansible runs with no network at all
Playbooks finish instantly no matter how many machines
items.explanation: A hand-written script often is not safe to re-run, whereas a declarative playbook checks and converges to the desired state each time. (Why that re-run safety works is the next lesson: idempotency.)

lessons.title: Idempotency: Why Re-Running Is Always Safe
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Here is the property that makes tools like Ansible safe and trustworthy: you can run the same playbook again and again, and nothing bad happens. The fancy word for this is **idempotent** - and it is worth understanding clearly, because it is *why* IaC works.

**Build.** Picture a **thermostat**. You set it to 70 degrees once. It does not keep pumping out heat forever; it checks the room and only acts when the temperature is off, then stops. Setting it to 70 a second time changes nothing - the room is already 70. That is the idea of a **desired state**: you declare the target, and the system keeps things matching it.

**Idempotent** means: doing the operation again gives the *same end result* as doing it once. A good Ansible task works exactly like the thermostat. For each item it first **checks**: "Is this already true?" If yes, it leaves it alone (a **no-op** - it does nothing). If no, it fixes just that one thing. So:

- **First run:** the machine is not set up, so Ansible makes changes.
- **Second run (same playbook):** everything already matches, so Ansible reports "ok" and changes nothing.

Contrast that with a naive shell script that blindly runs `echo "..." >> config` every time: run it three times and you get three duplicate lines. *That* is not idempotent. Because Ansible describes desired state and checks before acting, re-running is always safe - you do not have to remember whether you already ran it.

**Recap.** Idempotent = running it again lands you in the same end state, with no harm. The tool checks first and fixes only what is off, like a thermostat holding a set temperature. That re-run safety is the reason a written, re-applied artifact can be trusted as the source of truth.

items.prompt: What does "idempotent" mean?
choices: Running an operation again gives the same end result as running it once - no extra changes
An operation that can only ever be run a single time
An operation that runs faster each time you repeat it
An operation that requires a password
items.explanation: Idempotent means repeating the operation does not change the result beyond the first application. Run it once or ten times - the end state is the same.

items.prompt: Which everyday device best illustrates idempotency / desired state?
choices: A stopwatch that counts up forever
A thermostat you set to 70 degrees, which then just holds the room at 70
A light switch that toggles randomly
A clock that loses time
items.explanation: A thermostat holds a declared target: setting 70 again does nothing because the room is already 70. That is exactly desired-state, idempotent behavior.

items.prompt: You run a playbook that says nginx should be installed and running. It already is. What happens on this run?
choices: It errors because nginx is already there
It reports the tasks as 'ok' and makes no changes (a no-op)
It reinstalls nginx to be safe
It uninstalls and reinstalls nginx
items.explanation: Because the desired state already holds, Ansible checks, sees nothing to do, and reports 'ok' with no changes. Idempotent tasks do not redo work that is already done.

items.prompt: On the FIRST run against a brand-new machine, what does an idempotent playbook do?
choices: Nothing, because idempotent means it never changes anything
It makes the changes needed to reach the desired state (installs, configures, starts things)
It only prints a warning
It deletes the machine
items.explanation: Idempotent does not mean 'never changes anything' - it means re-running causes no extra changes. The first run still does the real work to reach the desired state.

items.prompt: How does an idempotent task decide whether to act?
choices: It always acts, every time
It first checks 'is this already true?' and only changes the thing if it is not
It flips a coin
It acts only on the first Tuesday of the month
items.explanation: The check-then-act pattern is the heart of idempotency: verify the current state, and fix only what differs from the desired state.

items.prompt: What is a "no-op"?
choices: An operation that fails with an error
An operation that does nothing because the desired state already holds
A brand-new operating system
A command that runs twice as fast
items.explanation: A no-op ('no operation') is when the tool finds nothing to change and so does nothing. On a fully-configured machine, most tasks become no-ops.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT idempotent?
choices: A task that ensures a package is 'present' (installed)
A task that ensures a service is 'started'
A script that appends a line to a config file every single time it runs
A task that ensures a config file has an exact, specified content
items.explanation: Blindly appending a line each run keeps adding duplicates, so repeated runs change the result - not idempotent. Ensuring 'installed', 'started', or 'exact content' all converge to one stable state.

items.prompt: Why does idempotency make a written playbook trustworthy as the 'source of truth'?
choices: Because it makes the playbook run faster
Because you can safely re-apply it any time to guarantee the machine matches the description, without fear of damage from re-running
Because it encrypts the playbook
Because it means the playbook never needs to change
items.explanation: Re-run safety means the artifact can always be re-applied to reassert the desired state. That reliability is exactly what lets the code, not someone's memory, be the source of truth.

items.prompt: A machine has drifted - someone manually stopped nginx. You re-run the idempotent playbook. What happens?
choices: Nothing, because idempotent tools never change anything
It notices nginx is stopped (off from desired state) and starts it again, fixing just that
It errors and refuses to run
It reinstalls the whole operating system
items.explanation: Idempotency plus desired-state means re-running corrects drift: it acts only on what is now wrong (nginx stopped) and brings it back to the declared state. This is a key reason to re-run regularly.

lessons.title: The Production Landscape: Provision, Ship, Watch
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You now have the core: SSH, Infrastructure as Code, Ansible, and idempotency. In a real production setting, a few more tools sit around that core. The goal here is *not* to master them - it is to recognize the words and know where each fits on the map.

**Build.** Think of running software in production as four stages, plus a couple of things that cut across all of them.

1. **Provision** - create the machines and cloud resources in the first place (the servers, networks, addresses). The well-known tool here is **Terraform**. Handy contrast: *Terraform creates the empty machines; Ansible sets up what is inside them.*

2. **Configure** - set up the inside of each machine: packages, files, services. That is **Ansible**, which you just learned.

3. **Ship code** - get new versions of your software built, tested, and deployed automatically. This is **CI/CD** (Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery): an automated pipeline that, on every code change, builds and tests the software - usually inside **containers** (lightweight, isolated bundles of an app and its dependencies) so each run is clean and repeatable. When you run *many* containers across many machines, a system called **Kubernetes** schedules and supervises them - it is the same declarative, desired-state idea as Ansible, but applied to a whole cluster.

4. **Watch** (called **observability**) - once it is running, you must be able to see whether it is healthy. Three layers: **logs** (text messages the software writes, gathered from all machines into one searchable place instead of logging into each); **metrics** (numbers over time like CPU use or requests per second) - the common pairing is **Prometheus** to collect and store them and **Grafana** to draw the dashboards; and for the deepest, live questions ("what exactly is this program doing right now?"), **eBPF** tooling inspects the running kernel safely (covered in its own module).

Two things cut across everything: **Secrets** - passwords, keys, tokens - must be kept in a locked **vault** and *never written into the code or checked into version control*. And **scheduled jobs** that must run on a clock use either classic **cron** or **systemd timers** (covered in the systemd module; timers add logging and missed-run recovery).

**Recap.** The landscape: Terraform provisions, Ansible configures, CI/CD ships (via containers, scaled by Kubernetes), and observability (logs + Prometheus/Grafana metrics + eBPF) watches. Secrets live in a vault, never in code. One idea ties it together: *describe what you want as code; let tools make reality match, automatically and repeatably.*

items.prompt: What is the difference in job between Terraform and Ansible?
choices: They are the same tool with different names
Terraform creates the machines/cloud resources (provisioning); Ansible sets up what is inside them (configuring)
Terraform watches dashboards; Ansible stores passwords
Terraform is for Windows; Ansible is for Linux
items.explanation: A clean split: Terraform provisions (makes the empty machines and networks exist), then Ansible configures the inside (packages, files, services). Provision first, then configure.

items.prompt: What does CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery) do?
choices: It encrypts hard drives
It is an automated pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys your software whenever the code changes
It is a type of network cable
It manually emails code to teammates
items.explanation: CI/CD automates the path from a code change to running software: build, test, deploy. It is how teams ship frequently and reliably instead of by hand.

items.prompt: Why do CI/CD pipelines often build and test inside containers?
choices: Containers make the code run on a phone
Containers give a clean, repeatable environment so the build/test behaves the same every time and on every machine
Containers delete the test results automatically
Containers are required by law
items.explanation: A container pins the environment, so a build/test is reproducible - the same on a laptop, the CI server, and production. That kills 'but it worked on my machine' problems.

items.prompt: How does Kubernetes relate to the declarative/desired-state idea from Ansible?
choices: It abandons that idea entirely
It applies the same idea at cluster scale: you declare the desired state (e.g. 3 copies of this container) and it keeps reality matching, restarting and rescheduling as needed
It only manages a single machine
It replaces SSH with a faster cable
items.explanation: Kubernetes is declarative across many machines: you state the desired end state and a control loop continuously reconciles reality to match - the same 'declare and converge' philosophy as a playbook, scaled up.

items.prompt: In observability, what is the difference between logs and metrics?
choices: Logs are numbers; metrics are text
Logs are text messages the software writes; metrics are numbers tracked over time (like CPU use or requests per second)
They are identical
Logs run the software; metrics store passwords
items.explanation: Logs = text records of events. Metrics = numeric measurements over time. They answer different questions, which is why production setups collect both.

items.prompt: How do Prometheus and Grafana typically divide the work for metrics?
choices: Prometheus draws the dashboards; Grafana stores the data
Prometheus collects and stores the numeric metrics; Grafana draws the dashboards and graphs from them
Both only handle text logs
Neither has anything to do with metrics
items.explanation: The common pairing: Prometheus gathers and stores the time-series numbers; Grafana visualizes them as dashboards and alerts. Collect/store vs visualize.

items.prompt: Why is gathering logs from all machines into one searchable place better than logging into each machine to read them?
choices: It makes the logs run the software faster
You can search and compare events across the whole fleet at once, which does not scale if you must visit each machine by hand
It deletes the logs to save space
It is the only way logs can be written at all
items.explanation: Central log collection lets you query and correlate across many machines from one place. Logging into each box to grep does not scale and cannot easily compare across machines.

items.prompt: Where does eBPF fit among logs and metrics?
choices: It replaces all logging and metrics
It is the deepest, live layer - it safely inspects the running kernel to answer 'what exactly is this program doing right now?'
It is a type of password vault
It is an older tool that predates logs
items.explanation: eBPF answers live, low-level questions about a running system (syscalls, files, network events) that pre-written logs and metrics cannot. It sits below them as the deep-inspection layer.

items.prompt: What is the cardinal rule about secrets (passwords, keys, tokens)?
choices: Always write them directly into your code so the setup is self-contained
Keep them in a locked vault and never write them into the code or check them into version control
Email them to the whole team for safety
Store them in the dashboard title
items.explanation: Secrets must live in a vault and be injected at run time, never committed in code - even a private repository can leak. Plaintext secrets in version control is a classic, dangerous mistake.

items.prompt: For jobs that must run on a schedule (like a nightly backup), which two tools were mentioned?
choices: Terraform and Grafana
Classic cron and systemd timers
Prometheus and Kubernetes
SSH and YAML
items.explanation: Scheduling on Linux is done with classic cron or with systemd timers (covered in the systemd module). Timers add benefits like logging and catching runs missed during downtime.

items.prompt: What single idea ties this whole landscape together?
choices: Always do everything by hand for control
Describe what you want as code, and let tools make reality match it automatically and repeatably
Avoid writing anything down
Use as many different tools as possible for their own sake
items.explanation: From provisioning to shipping to watching, the unifying thread is declarative, code-driven automation: state the desired result and let tools converge reality to it - reproducible, not remembered.
